rule: Encrypt all data at rest using AWS KMS or service-native SSE (S3 SSE-KMS, EBS/RDS/DynamoDB encryption); prefer customer-managed keys for auditable rotation.
rationale: Default at-rest encryption protects data if storage media or snapshots are exposed and is required by most compliance baselines.
summary: Encrypt all data at rest (KMS / service-native SSE), preferring customer-managed keys.

rule: Enforce TLS for every connection and reject plaintext: terminate HTTPS at CloudFront/ALB/API Gateway and deny non-TLS access with an aws:SecureTransport: false bucket/endpoint policy.
rationale: TLS-only transport prevents eavesdropping and tampering on data in motion, including between the edge and origin.
summary: Enforce TLS in transit everywhere; terminate HTTPS at the edge and reject plaintext.

rule: Grant least-privilege IAM: scoped per-service roles with no wildcard (*) actions/resources, no long-lived access keys, and short-lived role credentials for workloads.
rationale: Minimal, scoped permissions limit the blast radius of a compromised credential and remove standing long-lived secrets.
summary: Least-privilege IAM: scoped per-service roles, no wildcards, no long-lived access keys.

rule: Enable S3 Block Public Access at the account level (and per bucket) so no bucket or object can be made public by ACL or policy.
rationale: Account-level Block Public Access is the single highest-value guard against the most common AWS data-leak: an accidentally public bucket.
summary: Enable S3 Block Public Access account-wide so no bucket or object can be made public.

rule: Place data stores (RDS, ElastiCache, OpenSearch) in private subnets with no public IP or internet route; reach them only from app-tier security groups.
rationale: Keeping the data tier off the public internet removes direct attack surface; access flows through the app tier only.
summary: Keep data stores in private subnets — no public IP or internet route, app-tier access only.

rule: Store credentials, API keys, and connection strings in AWS Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store (SecureString); never in code, env files in the image, or plaintext config.
rationale: Centralized secret storage gives rotation, fine-grained access, and audit, and keeps secrets out of source control and images.
summary: Store secrets in AWS Secrets Manager / SSM Parameter Store — never in code or images.

rule: Front public endpoints with CloudFront plus AWS WAF (managed rule groups + a rate-based rule); AWS Shield Standard applies automatically for L3/L4 DDoS.
rationale: An edge WAF with managed and rate-based rules blocks common exploits and volumetric abuse before they reach the origin.
summary: Front public endpoints with CloudFront + AWS WAF (managed + rate-based rules); Shield Standard for L3/L4.

rule: Enable CloudTrail (management + relevant data events) and turn on access logging for S3, CloudFront, ALB, and VPC Flow Logs.
rationale: Comprehensive audit and access logs are required to detect, investigate, and prove what happened during an incident.
summary: Enable CloudTrail plus S3/CloudFront/ALB access logs and VPC Flow Logs for audit.
